food_subgroup: Rice bowl set meal with grilled meat, fermented vegetables, egg, and soup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for kimchi beef yakiniku bowl. FOODON_00002809 (beef dish) used as closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Beef, cooked) used as closest parent; no specific code exists for yakiniku bowl composite dish with kimchi and rice.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for this composite Japanese-Korean fusion rice bowl dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately 550 g, comprising a rice bowl with grilled beef and kimchi (approximately 400 g), one half-boiled egg (approximately 50 g), and one cup of miso soup (approximately 100 g).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, short loin, top loin steak, boneless, separable lean only, trimmed to 0 inch fat, all grades, cooked, grilled (FDC ID 174032); White Rice cooked (FDC ID 169756); Kimchi (FDC ID 169999); Miso (FDC ID 172443); Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled (FDC ID 173424). Composite estimation weighted by serving proportion.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values estimated by weighted-average composition: steamed white rice ~45% by weight, grilled beef slices ~30%, kimchi ~12%, half-boiled egg ~9%, miso soup contribution ~4%. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries used for each component. Energy cross-checked using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. White rice (boiled) GI = 64 (glucose reference). Composite GI estimated from white rice as dominant glycemic component (~45% by weight, ~75% of glycemic carbohydrate load).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kimchi beef yakiniku bowl. GI estimated at 64 based on white rice (boiled) as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source (GI=64, University of Sydney GI Database). Protein and fat from beef and egg, and acidity from kimchi, may moderately attenuate postprandial glucose response; however, white rice GI is retained as a conservative estimate. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (64 × 28.6) / 100 ≈ 18.3, where available carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 18.5 × 5.5 × 0.85 ≈ 28.6 g net glycemic carbohydrate after fibre adjustment.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from kimchi, miso soup, and yakiniku sauce
hypertension.guidance: One serving of this combo provides an estimated 2,310 mg sodium (~550 g × 420 mg/100 g), which exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium miso soup, limit kimchi portion, and consider this dish an occasional meal rather than a daily choice.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by white rice as dominant carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 64 (medium) and a glycemic load of approximately 18 per serving, this dish poses a moderate postprandial glucose challenge; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of rice, note that kimchi's acidity and the protein and fat content from beef and egg may partially attenuate glucose response, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables where possible.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and cholesterol from egg, combined with high sodium from fermented and sauce components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each serving contributes approximately 7.2 g saturated fat and 341 mg cholesterol alongside high sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption, choose leaner beef cuts where available, and be aware that the high sodium load from kimchi and miso soup may adversely affect blood pressure management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kimchi Beef Yakiniku Bowl Combo is a Japanese-style rice bowl dish (donburi) featuring thinly sliced beef marinated and grilled in a sweet-savory yakiniku sauce (typically soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, garlic, and sesame oil), served over steamed Japanese short-grain white rice and topped with fermented Korean kimchi. The combo set includes a half-boiled egg (or alternative side with top-up) and a cup of miso soup or a beverage. The dish is popularised in Singapore by Japanese fast-casual chain Sukiya and similar outlets. Yakiniku-style beef is tender, slightly caramelised, and umami-rich from the soy-based marinade; kimchi adds a spicy, tangy, and pungent fermented contrast. The combination of Japanese donburi tradition with Korean kimchi reflects the cross-cultural food landscape of Singapore's Japanese fast-food segment. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, protein-containing meal (~814 kcal per 550 g serving) with significant sodium content from kimchi, miso, and yakiniku sauce. The medium glycemic index (~64) is driven by white rice. The dish contains soy, wheat, egg, sesame, and potential fish allergens from dashi-based miso soup. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or halal-certified diets without modification. Clinically, the high sodium content (~2,310 mg per serving) is the primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish as served commercially. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of USDA FoodData Central component entries (beef, white rice, kimchi, egg, miso). GI is estimated from white rice as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source using the University of Sydney GI Database. Serving size is estimated based on typical Sukiya-style donburi combo portions. Confidence is medium rather than low because all component data come from approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese fast-casual donburi combo portions (rice bowl ~400 g + egg ~50 g + miso soup ~100 g); actual restaurant portion may vary. 2. Sodium estimate of 420 mg/100 g is high due to kimchi (~700–900 mg/100 g sodium), miso soup (~400–600 mg/100 g sodium), and yakiniku sauce (~800–1,200 mg/100 g sodium) contributions; this is a weighted composite estimate. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not directly measured for this dish; values are approximate. 4. No HPB Singapore Foodo entry exists for this dish. 5. The dish is not confirmed halal; Sukiya Singapore is not listed as a halal-certified outlet as of the knowledge cutoff. 6. Kimchi is a Korean fermented ingredient incorporated into a Japanese donburi format; the dish is classified as Japanese cuisine reflecting the restaurant category. 7. GI of 64 is assigned based on white rice (boiled) as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database; the actual composite GI may be slightly lower due to protein, fat, and acidity effects from beef, egg, and kimchi. 8. GL per serving = (64 × 28.6) / 100 ≈ 18.3, where net glycemic carbohydrate per serving is estimated at 28.6 g after dietary fibre adjustment across the 550 g serving.
